coredata.dc:title: Electron-beam radiation-induced ultrafast synthesis of highly conjugated carbon quantum dots with excellent fluorescence intensity
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chengcheng
Lv, Kun
Yu, Jiangtao
Que, Xueyan
Liu, Jialei
Ouyang, Xingnan
Li, Moyan
Tian, Yaru
Song, Jian
Wang, Jinxing
Li, Jianming
coredata.dc:description: A steadily increasing research interest has been devoted to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a highly promising visible-light-triggered material. However, the commonly used hydrothermal reaction method to produce CQDs is usually a lengthy and tedious process, which requires elevated temperatures. Herein, we report for the first time the rapid synthesis of highly conjugated CQDs using lignin as the precursor activated by high-energy electron-beam radiation from an electron accelerator at different doses (0–400 kGy). Lignin was used as the raw material for the generation of lignin fractions with different molecular weights by employing electron-beam radiation under ambient conditions within minutes, and the CQDs were directly obtained via centrifugation and dialysis. The CQDs obtained at an irradiation dose of 250 kGy have the highest fluorescence intensity. At a radiation dose of 250 kGy, the electron beam depolymerizes lignin into numerous small molecule fragments; these low-molecular-weight units can form a large number of highly conjugated carbon core structures (C=C), leading to a remarkable enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs have a positive effect on tomato photosynthesis at a concentration as low as 250 mg/L. This study establishes a new strategy for the large-scale synthesis of CQDs with higher fluorescence intensities, and the findings provide valuable ideas for future studies of electron-beam radiation-assisted synthesis of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electron beam
Radiation
Synthesis
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of the metabolites and transcriptome of tomato leaves
Effect of EB radiation on the properties of lignin
Mechanism of the EB radiation-induced synthesis of highly conjugated CQDs
Effect of the CQDs on the photosynthesis of tomato plants

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: North West Agriculture and Forestry University and Shaanxi Postdoctoral Foundation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank a Faculty Startup fund of North West Agriculture and Forestry University and Shaanxi Postdoctoral Foundation ( 2023BSHYDZZ88 ) for the financial support. Authors would like to thank the instrument shared platform of college of food science & engineering of NWAFU for the assistance in the FT-IR analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of the lignin and EB-irradiated lignin under different irradiation dose (a); XRD spectra of the lignin and EB-irradiated lignin under different irradiation dose (b).

XPS full-spectral scan of lignin and EB-irradiated lignin under different irradiation dose (a); C 1 s high-resolution XPS fitting spectra of lignin and EB-irradiated lignin under different irradiation dose (b); O 1 s high-resolution XPS of lignin and EB-irradiated lignin under different irradiation dose (c).
The luminescence intensity of CQDs-EB under different irradiation dose (a); The luminescence intensity of CQDs-EBH under different irradiation dose (b).
FTIR spectra of CQDs-EB (a); FTIR spectra of CQDs-EBH (b); XRD spectra of CQDs-EB (c); XRD spectra of CQDs-EBH (d).

XPS full-spectral scan of CQDs-EB under different irradiation dose (a); C 1 s high-resolution XPS fitting spectra of CQDs-EB under different irradiation dose (b); O 1 s high-resolution XPS fitting spectra of CQDs-EB under different irradiation dose (c).

XPS full-spectral scan of CQDs-EBH under different irradiation dose (a); C 1 s high-resolution XPS fitting spectra of CQDs-EBH under different irradiation dose (b); O 1 s high-resolution XPS fitting spectra of CQDs-EBH under different irradiation dose (c).
The possible mechanisms of superior fluorescence performance of prepared CQDs-EB-250.

Photosynthetic parameters: the net photosynthesis rate (Pn) of tomato leaves under different cultivation days (a), stomatal conductance (Cd) of tomato leaves under different cultivation days (b), intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) of tomato leaves under different cultivation days (c), transpiration rate (Tr) of tomato leaves under different cultivation days (d).
Hierarchical clustering analysis performed on the metabolites.
Hierarchical clustering analysis performed on the genes.
Molecular weight statistics of lignin samples.

head.ce:title: Electron-beam radiation-induced ultrafast synthesis of highly conjugated carbon quantum dots with excellent fluorescence intensity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Horticulture, North West Agriculture and Forestry University, Yangling 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: North West Agriculture and Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Horticulture, North West Agriculture and Forestry University, Yangling 712100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yangling Hesheng Irradiation Technologies Co., Ltd., Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yangling Hesheng Irradiation Technologies Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yangling Hesheng Irradiation Technologies Co., Ltd., Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A steadily increasing research interest has been devoted to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a highly promising visible-light-triggered material. However, the commonly used hydrothermal reaction method to produce CQDs is usually a lengthy and tedious process, which requires elevated temperatures. Herein, we report for the first time the rapid synthesis of highly conjugated CQDs using lignin as the precursor activated by high-energy electron-beam radiation from an electron accelerator at different doses (0–400 kGy). Lignin was used as the raw material for the generation of lignin fractions with different molecular weights by employing electron-beam radiation under ambient conditions within minutes, and the CQDs were directly obtained via centrifugation and dialysis. The CQDs obtained at an irradiation dose of 250 kGy have the highest fluorescence intensity. At a radiation dose of 250 kGy, the electron beam depolymerizes lignin into numerous small molecule fragments; these low-molecular-weight units can form a large number of highly conjugated carbon core structures (C=C), leading to a remarkable enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs have a positive effect on tomato photosynthesis at a concentration as low as 250 mg/L. This study establishes a new strategy for the large-scale synthesis of CQDs with higher fluorescence intensities, and the findings provide valuable ideas for future studies of electron-beam radiation-assisted synthesis of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are a new member of the nanomaterials family, have recently garnered significant attention and become a research hotspot for improving plant growth and resistance due to their small size, superior fluorescence properties, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. The most common synthesis methods of CQDs include microwave [3], chemical oxidation [2], electrochemical oxidation [4], and hydrothermal synthesis [5]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal treatment based on small organic molecules, such as citric acid, o-phenylenediamine, and m-phenylenediamine, has been used by many researchers for preparing CQDs [6]. These small organic molecules are structurally simple and can easily undergo hydrothermal reactions, mainly involving dehydration, polymerization, aromatization, and condensation [7]. However, such a synthesis process usually requires a relatively long time (>48 h), elevated temperatures, a closed system, and high autogenous pressures. Therefore, the synthesis of CQDs based on traditional methods is quite energy consuming when large-scale production is considered. Developing new CQD synthesis methods that are environmentally friendly and highly efficient (in terms of both time and energy) remains a research goal in this area.

As an industrial by-product, lignin is inexpensive and widely available. Synthesis of CQDs using lignin as the carbon source not only realizes the resource utilization of waste but also aligns with the concepts of green chemistry and sustainable development and lowering the carbon footprint. The use of low-cost lignin as the precursor for the synthesis of CQDs is attractive due to its high carbon content and abundance of aromatic rings and reactive sites, such as aliphatic and aromatic hydroxyl groups as well as carboxyl groups [8]. These characteristics facilitate the carbonization reaction and surface functionalization of lignin when utilized for the formation of CQDs. In the hydrothermal reaction process, the hydrogen ions dissociated from water first attack the ether bonds of lignin to form a large number of lignin fragments, which consist of hydrophobic aromatic structures and hydrophilic hydroxyl groups. Furthermore, these fragments condense to form primary clusters and then transform into primary aromatic clusters through dehydration, polymerization, and condensation reactions with a prolonged reaction time [8]. Finally, these primary aromatic clusters undergo rapid π–π bond accumulation, an increase in sp2 hybridization, and aromatic polymerization to form CQDs; however, these processes require a high temperature and long reaction times [8]. To summarize, the depolymerization of lignin macromolecules is a key step for the formation of lignin CQDs, and the degree of depolymerization is closely related to the molecular weight of lignin.

Electron-beam (EB) radiation is a unique energy source that has found extensive utilization in many areas of basic research and industrial production. Since the energy of EBs can be much higher than that of chemical bonds, they can very efficiently activate all existing species in a chemical reaction [9]. EB radiation has gradually become an effective means to develop new materials with excellent properties or to modify conventional materials [9]. EB radiation uses a high energy to generate free-radical active sites on substrates, which in turn trigger the depolymerization of monomers at the active sites to generate short chains. This process exhibits many advantages, including ultrafast synthesis rate, ease of operation, high energy efficiency, and wide applicability.

Although CQD synthesis via one-step EB irradiation has yet to be reported, it can be speculated that EB irradiation of lignin may be a feasible method to depolymerize and carbonize lignin to form lignin-based CQDs through the mechanism described above. In this study, for the first time, a novel method for the rapid synthesis of CQDs based on EB radiation is proposed. The possible mechanism of EB radiation in the rapid synthesis of CQDs is discussed. The pre-irradiated lignin fragments are also used to hydrothermally prepare CQDs to compare their fluorescence intensities. The excellent performance of the synthesized CQDs in enhancing the photosynthesis of tomato plants is verified. Our results not only provide a new route to enhance the synthesis efficiency of CQDs but also lay the groundwork for safer and more sustainable applications of these CQD materials in agriculture.

Lignin was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and was exposed to EB irradiation at different doses (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, and 400 kGy) at Yangling Hesheng Irradiation Technologies Co., Ltd. to induce suitable modifications. Based on the various irradiation does, the irradiated lignin samples are denoted as follows: Lignin-0, Lignin-50, Lignin-100, Lignin-150, Lignin-200, Lignin-250, Lignin-300, Lignin-350, and Lignin-400, respectively.

Next, 1.2 g of irradiated lignin was fully mixed with 100 mL of distilled water, and the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min; the supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22-μm filter. The filtrate was subsequently transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da for 12 h of dialysis. Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was collected, and the CQDs were obtained through freeze-drying and were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis and application. The prepared CQDs (CQDs-EB) are named CQDs-EB-0, CQDs-EB-50, CQDs-EB-150, CQDs-EB-200, CQDs-EB-250, CQDs-EB-300, CQDs-EB-350, and CQDs-EB-400 according to the various irradiation doses listed above, respectively.

Subsequently, the solution containing 1.2 g of irradiated lignin dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel high-pressure reaction autoclave. The autoclave was sealed tightly and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22-μm filter. The filtrate was subsequently transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da for 12 h of dialysis. Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was collected, and the CQDs were obtained through freeze-drying. The prepared CQDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis and application. The hydrothermally synthesized CQDs (CQDs-EBH) are named CQDs-EBH-0, CQDs-EBH-50, CQDs-EBH-100, CQDs-EBH-150, CQDs-EBH-200, CQDs-EBH-250, CQDs-EBH-300, CQDs-EBH-350, and CQDs-EBH-400 according to the various irradiation doses listed above, respectively.

The relative molecular weight of the irradiated lignin fractions was measured using gel permeation chromatography (GPC; Rid-20 A, Japan) with Tetrahydrofuran as the mobile phase. The morphology of all CQDs was investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S, USA). The crystalline phases were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Holland. The X'pert diffractometer used Cu-Kα radiation, and the scanning speed was 0.03° s−1. The elemental state of the samples was examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-ALPHA, UK) with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (150 W, 15 kV, 1486.6 eV). The functional groups of the powders and the dried films (1 %) were identified using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet NICOLET IS50, USA) in the transmission mode. All spectra were recorded within the range of 4000–400 cm−1 at a resolution of 4.0 cm−1. The Raman spectra of the synthesized CQDs were acquired from 400 to 3000 cm−1 using a Ram HR Evolution spectrometer (Andor SR-500i, UK) with 532 nm as the excitation light. The fluorescence emission spectra were collected from 220 to 460 nm using a fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu F-7000, Japan).

CQDs-EB-250 was dispersed in ultrapure water under sonication for 30 min before the tomato cultivation. Tomato seedling growth was performed in an illumination incubator for 21 days with a photoperiod of 12 h (30,000 lx) and a temperature of 25 °C ± 2 °C. The tomato seedlings were cultivated in a culture pot (10 cm in diameter × 10 cm in height) filled with mixed media consisting of peat. Plants subjected to different treatments were watered with the same volume of Hoagland's solution and underwent the same cultivation management process. The tomato plants were sprayed with 250-mg/L CQDs-EB-250 every 3 days throughout the cultivation period. Each cultivation treatment has six replicates. Sampling was conducted for different cultivation times (1, 11, 16, and 21 days). An LI-6800 portable photosynthetic analyzer (Lincoln, USA) was used to measure the net photosynthesis rate (P n), stomatal conductance (C d), intercellular CO2 concentration (C i), and transpiration rate (T r) of the harvested leaves for different cultivation times (1, 11, 16, and 21 days). Three separate measurements were taken every 2 h, and the corresponding means and standard deviations (error bars) were calculated.

The harvested leaves at the end of cultivation (21 days) were washed three times with ultrapure water and a PBS solution; they were then quickly frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored in a refrigerator at −80 °C for metabolic and transcriptome testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the metabolites and transcriptome of tomato leaves
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insights into the effect of CQDs-EB-250 on tomato growth, the changes in the metabolites in tomato leaves exposed to 250-mg/L CQDs-EB-250 for 21 days and the control group were examined. The tomato leaves were washed with ultrapure water, quickly frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored in a refrigerator at −80 °C. A 50-mg solid sample of tomato leaves was placed in a 2.0-mL centrifuge tube. Two small steel beads and 1 mL of pre-cooled 50 % methanol were added to the centrifuge tube and immersed in liquid nitrogen for rapid freezing for 5 min. Then, the centrifuge tube was placed in a high-throughput tissue grinder and ground at 55 Hz for 60 s. The above steps were repeated two or three times. Furthermore, the samples were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm organic-phase filter and transferred to the LC injection vial for LC-MS analysis. A thermo Orbitrap Exploris 120 mass spectrometer was used to collect the DDA mass spectrometry data in the positive and negative ions modes with the Xcalibur software (version: 4.7, Thermo).

The raw LC-MS data were processed using baseline filtering, peak identification, integration, retention time correction, peak alignment, and normalization using the Compound Discoverer™ 3.3 software. The self-built library PSNGM Database, mzCloud online library (https://www.mzcloud.org/), LIPID MAPS (https://www.lipidmaps.org/), HMDB (https://hmdb.ca/), MoNA (https://mona.fiehnlab.ucdavis.edu/), and NIST_2020_MSMS spectral library were used to identify the compounds. The differences between the control and exposed groups were analyzed using the orthogonal projections to latent structures discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA) model. Substances with R 2 < 1 and p < 0.05 were considered differential metabolites. Biological pathway analysis was performed based on the LC-MS data using the clusterProfiler software (V4.6.0). Extraction of the metabolites and UHPLC-HESI-MS/MS analysis were performed by Personal Bioinformatics Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Each treatment was performed three times.

Total RNA was isolated from the soybean leaves in the 250-mg/L CQDs-EB-250 sample and control groups using a NEBNext Ultra II RNA Library Prep Kit (New England Biolabs Inc.; Ipswich, Massachusetts, USA) and pretreated with gDNA Eraser (Agilent Technologies Inc., California, USA, 5067–4626). Qualified RNA was used to prepare sequencing libraries, and Illumina sequencing was performed using an Illumina HiSeq 2000 platform from Personal Bioinformatics Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Each treatment was performed three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of EB radiation on the properties of lignin
ce:section.ce:para: Lignin is a complex amorphous polymer with a three-dimensional network structure formed by three types of phenylpropane units connected by an ether bond and a carbon–carbon bond [10]. EB radiation can be used to efficiently depolymerize lignin to different lower-molecular-weight fractions, as evidenced by the molecular weight distributions (Table 1 ). The weight-average molecular weight (M w) and the number-average molecular weight (M n) of lignin are 741 and 575 g mol−1, respectively. Eight lignin fractions with decreased molecular weights were named as follows: Lignin-50 (M w = 623 g mol−1), Lignin-100 (M w = 641 g mol−1), Lignin-150 (M w = 627 g mol−1), Lignin 200 (M w = 604 g mol−1), Lignin-250 (M w = 605 g mol−1), Lignin-300 (M w = 630 g mol−1), Lignin-350 (M w = 586 g mol−1), and Lignin-400 (M w = 639 g mol−1). Their M n are 515, 524, 517, 507, 507, 520, 487, and 523 g mol−1, respectively. It can be concluded that the molecular weight of the lignin fractions is smaller at an irradiation dose of 200, 250, or 350 kGy.

FTIR spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, XRD, and XPS measurements were conducted to determine the chemical composition of EB-irradiated lignin, and the results were compared with those of lignin (Lignin-0). As shown in Fig. 1a, the peak at around 3434 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the OH group, the peak at 1600 cm−1 corresponds to the CO bending vibrations, and the peaks at 1460 and 1120 cm−1 can be assigned to the CC and CH stretching vibrations, respectively (Fumoto et al., 2022). These peaks are related to lignin, and they are present also in EB-irradiated lignin, demonstrating that their monolithic skeletons have similar chemical groups. The EB-irradiated lignin samples were further analyzed via Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S1-a). The lignin and EB-irradiated lignin samples show almost identical Raman spectra. Sharp diffraction peaks in the lignin and EB-irradiated lignin samples are observed in the powder XRD patterns (Fig. 1b), indicating their excellent graphite-like crystallization (Rai et al. [11], 20). The two peaks at 23.54° and 44.52° correspond to the (002) reflection facets of graphite (ICDD #96–901-1578), while the diffraction peaks centered at 2θ = 18.71°, 27.26°, 31.73°, 32.77°, 33.45°, 34.36°, 45.02°, 48.42°, and 52.81° correspond to the (001), (111), (002), (020), (021), (200), (022), (003), and (220) exposed facets of graphite (ICDD #96–901-2232). It can be seen from the XRD patterns that the diffraction peak intensity of the (002) crystal planes gradually decreases as the EB irradiation dose rises from 0 to 250 kGy and then increases slightly as the EB irradiation dose rises from 250 to 400 kGy. The diffraction peak intensity of the (020), (021) (200), and (220) crystal planes also shows a similar trend. This may be because when increasing the EB irradiation dose from 0 to 250 kGy, the molecular weight of lignin gradually decreases, and the intermolecular force becomes weaker, resulting in the nucleation of dislocations or microscopic deformations in the crystal planes. As the EB irradiation dose increases from 250 to 400 kGy, the entanglement density between molecules increases, that is, crosslinking is established at higher EB irradiation doses (>250 kGy), and parts of the molecules are reordered to increase the degree of crystallinity of the crystal planes. This conclusion is supported by the observed trend in the molecular weight: As the EB irradiation dose increases from 0 to 250 kGy, the molecular weight gradually decreases. As the EB irradiation dose increases from 250 to more than 300 kGy, the molecular weight fluctuates, but it increases overall, indicating that crosslinking is established within the lignin molecules under a high EB irradiation dose.

We next studied the chemical composition of the lignin and EB-irradiated lignin samples through comprehensive XPS analysis (Fig. 2 ). In the entire XPS spectra (Fig. 2a), only the C 1 s (284.9 eV) and O 1 s (531.4 eV) peaks in different ratios are observed, suggesting that the lignin and EB-irradiated lignin samples are mainly composed of C and O. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum shows three peaks corresponding to CC (284.1 eV), CO (285.5 eV), and CO (287.5 eV) in both lignin and EB-irradiated lignin (Fig. 2b). The CC peak can be attributed to the guaiac-based structure units, whereas the CO and CO peaks are due to the carboxylate group and ester [11]. From the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c), the peak at 530.6 eV can be attributed to C–OH in the hydroxyl group [12]; the adsorption peaks at 532.3 and 534.3 eV correspond to CO and –COO, respectively, which can be attributed to the carboxyl group or the ether bonding linkage between the guaiac-based structure units [13]. The EB-irradiated lignin samples show no difference in the types of surface functional groups with respect to those found in lignin, but there are some differences in the content of these functional groups. This suggests that EB radiation can be considered as a means to separate lignin samples with different molecular weights without affecting the chemical bonds of lignin.

Lignin is rich in aromatic ring structures, aliphatic and aromatic hydroxyl groups, and quinone groups [10]. The oxygen-containing functional groups in lignin, such as the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, can significantly change the surface chemical properties of the synthesized materials. The presence of these functional groups can enhance the hydrophilicity or lipophilicity of the surface of the synthetic material, thus improving its adaptability and reactivity in agricultural systems. Moreover, the CC bond in lignin has a high reactivity due to the π electron in the double bond, which acts as a key intermediate in many organic reactions, especially in the synthesis of photothermal conversion materials [14]. The presence of CC bonds also affects the spatial structure of molecules, promoting the formation of more stable compounds. Although the carbonization of lignin precursors via EB radiation to fabricate CQDs is an unexplored topic, lignin with its heterogenicity and complex structure might be a potential raw material for the synthesis of CQDs with a high fluorescence intensity. Since the reduction of the molecular weight of lignin via irradiation is a physical process, the structural and compositional advantages of EB-irradiated lignin might be largely inherited by lignin-based CQDs. Therefore, the difference in functional group content might have a significant effect on the properties of the obtained lignin-based CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the EB radiation-induced synthesis of highly conjugated CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-EB were obtained through one-step centrifugation and dialysis using lignin or EB-irradiated lignin as the precursor. For comparison, CQDs-EBH were also produced via the hydrothermal treatment using lignin or EB-irradiated lignin as the raw material. The optical properties of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH were investigated via photoluminescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 , all synthesized CQDs show strong absorption peaks near 230 nm, with a maximum emission at 459 nm, which are induced by the π–π* leaps of the conjugate effect in the synthesized CQDs [15]. Benzene ring stacking in lignin or EB-irradiated lignin can induce a strong π–π conjugation effect when molecules aggregate, which endow them with an excellent light absorption and photoconversion capacity [11]. Therefore, regardless of whether lignin or irradiated lignin is used as the precursor for preparing CQDs, CQDs with good light-transfer properties can be obtained via dialysis and centrifugation due to the fact that they inherit the structural characteristics of the raw material.

It is worth noting that the luminescence intensity of the prepared CQDs is different. Among the various CQDs-EB samples, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-EB-250 is the highest; on the other hand, among the different CQDs-EBH samples, the highest fluorescence intensity is found in CQDs-EBH-200. These results confirm the significant influence of the radiation dose to which lignin is exposed on the fluorescence properties of the subsequent reaction CQD products. Interestingly, the luminous intensity of all CQDs after the hydrothermal reaction is significantly lower than that of the non-hydrothermal CQDs. For example, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-EB-250 is significantly higher than that of CQDs-EBH-200, which may be related to the strength of the conjugation effect for each CQD structure (more details are provided in XPS analysis). At a concentration of 1 mg mL−1, all CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH show tawny colors under sunlight, and all CQDs emit bright blue or blue–green fluorescence when illuminated with a 365-nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp (Fig. S2), showing superior up-conversion photoluminescence properties.

The key properties of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH were investigated, including their functional groups and microstructure morphologies. As shown in Fig. 4 , the FTIR spectra of CQDs-EB (Fig. 4a), CQDs-EBH (Fig. 4b), and lignin fractions (lignin and EB-irradiated lignin) (Fig. 1a) are almost similar albeit with different peak intensities. The peaks at around 3434 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of the OH group), 1600 cm−1 (CO bending vibrations), 1460 cm−1 (CC stretching vibrations), and 1050 cm−1 (CH stretching vibrations) [16] are still visible in the spectra of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH, and no new peaks can be observed when comparing with the spectra of lignin and EB-irradiated lignin. These results show that no new type of chemical bonding is formed during the synthesis of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH using lignin or EB-irradiated lignin as the raw material. This is further supported by the Raman spectroscopy results (Figs. S1b and c). The initial characteristic peaks (at around 1588 cm−1) of lignin and EB-irradiated lignin can be observed in all CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH samples. In the XRD patterns of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH (Fig. 4), all CQDs exhibit different broad peaks corresponding to the crystallographic diffraction of the standard graphite structure, indicating that the carbon core of the CQDs has an amorphous graphite structure [11]. Specifically, most of the initial characteristic peaks found in lignin and EB-irradiated lignin have disappeared in the XRD pattern of CQDs-EB, while the two broad peaks corresponding to the (001) and (111) exposed facets of graphite (ICQDD #96–901-2232) are still present (Fig. 4c). Interestingly, in the XRD pattern of CQDs-EBH, only a broad diffraction peak corresponding to the (002) reflection facets of graphite (ICDD #96–901-2232) remains (Fig. 4d). These results show that the structural damage caused by irradiation to the as-prepared CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH mainly occurs on the vanishing crystal faces; the formation of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EB is part of the transition process of lignin and EB-irradiated lignin from crystalline to amorphous during dialysis or the hydrothermal treatment.

The intrinsic mechanism for the altered luminescence intensity observed for different irradiation doses of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH was investigated by performing XPS measurements. The XPS spectra in Figs. 5a and 6a indicate that CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH mainly contain C and O elements. The fitting of the C 1 s peak in Figs. 5b and 6b shows that the surfaces of CQDs-EB and CQDs-EBH contain three groups: The peak at 284.5 eV is attributed to CC in graphitic carbon, the peak at 286.1 eV is characteristic of CO in carboxylates, and the peak at 288.5 eV represents CO in esters [11]. The XPS results demonstrate that the content of the conjugated bond (C=C) in CQDs-EB-250 is the highest (69.97 %) among the various CQDs-EB, indicating that CQDs-EB-250 possesses more conjugated domains and thus exhibits the best fluorescence performance. A high CC content was also observed in CQDs-EB-250 compared with the different CQDs-EBH samples; the CC content is even higher than that of CQDs-EBH-200 (66.91 %, which results in the highest fluorescence among the CQDs-EBH samples) (Fig. 6b). The combination of the XPS and luminescence intensity results shows that all fluorescent CQDs contain strong absorption groups with conjugated double bonds, and the larger the number of conjugated bonds, the higher the fluorescence efficiency. This is because the greater the number of conjugated CC bonds, the more pronounced the electron delocalization.

With the reduction in molecular weight, the formation of a large number of conjugated CC bonds is crucial to improve the luminous intensity of CQDs in this study. For example, the molecular weight of EB-irradiated lignin at an irradiation dose of 350 kGy is the lowest (M w and M n are 586 and 487 g mol−1, respectively); however, this sample has the lowest content of conjugated CC bonds (46.51 %, as shown in Fig. 5), leading to the lowest luminous intensity (Fig. 3). Compared with all samples, the highest amount of CC was found in Lignin-250 (64.24 %). It can be concluded that when the synthesis of CQDs is assisted by an optimal EB radiation energy, the formation of highly conjugated bonds is favored. Moreover, the variation in CC content in the CQDs is consistent with that in EB-irradiated lignin (Fig. 2), confirming the hypothesis that the CQDs inherit the structural properties of EB-irradiated lignin in Section 3.1. The high-resolution XPS O 1 s spectra comprise three peaks, as shown in Figs. 5c and 6c, where the peak at 530.6 eV can be attributed to C–O–C=O in the carboxyl group/ester; the adsorption peak occurring at 531.5 can be attributed to CO in the phenolic hydroxyl group and ether bonding; the peak located at 533.1 eV can be attributed to C–O–C in the ether bonding linkage between the guaiac-based structure units. These results show that the oxygen-containing functional groups of the CQDs and the raw materials (lignin or EB-irradiated lignin) are similar, and this plays an important role in the hydrophilicity or lipophilicity solubility of CQDs for use in agricultural systems.

The results unambiguously demonstrate that the properties of CQDs-EB-250 prepared within minutes by employing EB radiation are considerably superior to those of CQDs-EBH obtained via the traditional hydrothermal synthesis from the aspects of both material synthesis (time and energy consumption) and fluorescence performance. The possible mechanisms underlying superior fluorescence performance of prepared CQDs-EB-250 are illustrated in Fig. 7 . Firstly, the greater the number of conjugated CC bonds, the higher the luminous intensity of the CQDs. According to the molecular orbital theory, delocalized π bonds form bonding orbital bands and anti-bonding orbital bands, and the energy difference between the orbitals becomes smaller, that is, the smaller the energy difference between the empty orbital (π) with the lowest energy and the occupied orbital (π*) with the highest energy, the smaller the energy required for transition. Therefore, in highly conjugated CQDs, electron transfer is enhanced, the electron transition bandgap is reduced, and the light absorption capacity is increased. However, the high temperature and pressure characteristic of the hydrothermal process cause the unsaturated CC double bonds of CQDs-EB to break. Such a chemical process results in a more disordered arrangement of the atoms in CQD-EBH, as evidenced by the XRD results (Figs. 4c and d). The FTIR spectra of lignin and all synthesized CQDs are similar, proving that the active groups of lignin have been inherited by CQDs. Second, the reduction in molecular weight can enhance the π–π conjugation effect of lignin as well as promote the absorption of light energy and electronic transitions [17]. Third, there are active groups in the molecular structure of lignin, such as aromatic groups, phenol hydroxyl groups, alcohol hydroxyl groups, and carbon-based conjugated double bonds. The XPS results indicate the presence of CC, CO, CO, and –COO on the surface of the synthesized CQDs, and the high content of CC bonds is the key behind the excellent fluorescence intensity of CQDs-EB-250. Moreover, not only the surface oxygen-containing groups, including CO, –OH, and –COO, play a key role in improving hydrophilicity, but recent research shows that these oxygen-containing functional groups can combine with superoxide anions through weak interactions, such as static electricity, hydrogen bonds, and other van der Waals forces, facilitating electronic transitions [17].

The TEM images shown in Fig. S3 depict the spherical morphology of the uniformly dispersed CQDs of the samples CQDs-EB-0, CQDs-EB-250, CQDs-EBH-0, and CQDs-EB-200, which have the same lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. These results indicate the presence of a highly crystalline graphitic structure in all CQDs [8]. The average particle sizes of CQDs-EB-0, CQDs-EB-250, CQDs-EBH-0, and CQDs-EB-200 are 10.33 ± 3.78, 4.40 ± 1.75, 2.39 ± 0.65, and 3.50 ± 1.11 nm, respectively. These results demonstrate that the above CQDs have similar carbon core structures, which is consistent with the conclusions drawn from the optical properties of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the CQDs on the photosynthesis of tomato plants
ce:section.ce:para: The positive impact of different types of CQDs on plant photosynthesis due to their unique light harvesting ability has been widely reported [18]. However, to verify that the new CQDs-EB samples synthesized in this study have a similar effect on improving crop photosynthesis, the influence of the application of 250-mg/L CQDs-EB-250 on the tomato photosynthesis was assessed and compared with that of the control group. Fig. 8 displays the net photosynthesis rate (P n), stomatal conductance (C d), intercellular CO2 concentration (C i), and transpiration rate (T r) of tomato leaves under different cultivation times. The results show the same trend. With the extension of the cultivation time, all parameters increase following the same trend for each group. Significant differences emerge between the control and 250-mg/L CQDs-EB-250 groups. At any cultivation time, the photosynthetic capacity of the treated group is higher than that of the control group. For example, the tomato plants of the control group exhibit significantly lower P n (20.71 ± 0.78 μmol CO2 m−2 s−1), C d (0.38 ± 0.001 mol H2O m−2 s−1), C i (322.78 ± 3.22 μmol CO2 mol−1), and T r (5.38 ± 0.68 mol H2O m−2 s−1) than those of the treated group (35.52 ± 0.11 μmol CO2 m−2 s−1, 1.34 ± 0.01 mol H2O m−2 s−1, 369.76 ± 5.12 μmol CO2 mol−1, and 10.61 ± 0.18 mol H2O m−2 s−1) over a period of 21 days. The P n of the leaves is closely related to the C d of the plants, which is due to the fact that an increase in C d enhances the supply of C i, leading to an increase in T r; this is consistent with our experimental results.

The above results show that in agreement with most reports, the CQDs in this study also show the ability to perform up-conversion and convert UV light into photosynthetic blue light. The blue fluorescence overlaps with the absorption of chloroplasts; therefore, the higher the intensity of the blue light converted by the CQDs, the more significant the light capture capacity and plant photosynthesis. The increase in P n for the different cultivation periods investigated in this study is presented in Table S1. P n increases by 135.13 %, 129.43 %, 39.333 %, and 71.51 % after the different cultivation times (1, 11, 16, and 21 days) compared with the control group, respectively. To facilitate comparison, we list in Table S2 the effects of different types of CQDs on the P n of different plants reported in several previous studies. According to this table, the CQDs-EB-250 dose of 250 mg/L used in this study has a high P n. This is mostly due to the following factors: 1) Tomato leaves absorb CQDs through the epidermis and leaf stomata, which can be affected by the surface chemistry of the CQDs. The FTIR spectra of CQDs-EB (Fig. 4) reveal the tensile vibration of a large number of hydrophilic groups (e.g., OH and –COO), which can enter plant leaves easily due to their small size and surface hydrophilicity; 2) CQDs promote the efficient operation and coordinated transmission of photosynthetic electrons in the photosystem due to the large number of conjugated CC and affect the plant vitality by adjusting the efficiency of photosynthesis. This is also evinced by the significantly improved fluorescence intensity of CQDs-EB-250 compared with that of CQDs-EB-0.

Metabolic changes in tomato leaves at the molecular level were analyzed to explain the effects of CQDs on plant photosynthesis due to the accumulation of CQDs in the leaves. By performing orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA), the CQD-treated group can be clearly distinguished from the untreated control group (Fig. S4), suggesting that the CQDs induce changes in the metabolism of the tomato leaves. A total of 41 substances were selected as differential metabolites (9 upregulated and 32 downregulated), and these metabolites were analyzed using heatmap clustering (Fig. 9 and Table S3). Nine upregulated metabolites, including Pimpinellin, (S)–Methylmalonic acid semialdehyde, Methyl 4-hydroxycinnamate, ATEE, bis-desmethoxycurcumin, Latifolicinin C acid, EGLU, Leu-Val, and N-Acetylprocainamide, act as signal regulators of various biochemical pathways in plant cells, playing critical roles in plant growth and antioxidant activity [19–25]. Biopterin is significantly upregulated in the CQD-treated group, and it contributes to photoreception [26]. To explain the CQD-induced metabolic changes observed in the tomato leaves, the transcript levels of genes associated with the metabolic changes during tomato growth were examined. The transcript levels of these genes were markedly different for the CQD-treated group and the control group (Fig. S5), again suggesting that the CQDs influence the metabolism of tomato leaves. A total of 483 differentially expressed genes (121 upregulated and 362 downregulated) were identified (p < 0.05). The up- and downregulated genes were selected based on the KEGG database and are shown in a heatmap (Fig. 10 and Table S4). The results show that exogenous CQDs have a positive effect on tomato growth, increasing amino acid metabolism, carbohydrate metabolism, and terpenoids and polyketides metabolism, as well as strengthening the biosynthesis of other secondary metabolites (including tropane, piperidine and pyridine alkaloid biosynthesis, and phenylpropanoid biosynthesis). The accumulation of amino acids in leaves helps to induce the formation of strong hydrogen-bonded water around the proteins and protects the natural state of cellular protein polymers [27]. Carbohydrate metabolism contributes to the accumulation of biomass, and phenylpropanoids are essential components of a number of structural polymers, providing protection from UV light. Sugar is the main product of photosynthesis, which allows plants to accumulate energy and produce organic matter. When photosynthesis is intense, more amino sugar and nucleotide sugar are formed in the chloroplasts and transported to the cytoplasm to form more sucrose, which becomes the product of photosynthesis. Exogenous CQDs can also improve tomato signal transduction, which is conducive to improving tomato environmental adaptability and increasing yield.

EB irradiation induces molecular weight fractionation of lignin within minutes, followed by centrifugation and dialysis to yield CQDs. At 250 kGy irradiation, lignin depolymerization generates low-molecular-weight fragments that form highly conjugated carbon cores (C=C), resulting in maximum fluorescence intensity. CQDs at 250 mg/L concentration significantly enhance photosynthetic efficiency in tomato plants, suggesting potential in agricultural biotechnology. This work provides a valuable approach for synthesizing high-fluorescence CQDs and highlights the potential of electron-beam radiation in CQDs nanomaterial engineering. Our study shows that the synthesized conjugated CQDs play a positive role in enhancing plant photosynthesis and also explains the underlying mechanism of this enhancement at the molecular level. However, how highly conjugated CQDs are phagocytosed, migrated, and transformed by tomato cells, as well as their specific binding with cells, has not been fully explored, which will be a future research direction.

Chengcheng Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kun Lv: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiangtao Yu: Software, Resources, Methodology. Xueyan Que: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology. Jialei Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xingnan Ouyang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Moyan Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yaru Tian: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jian Song: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jinxing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jianming Li: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank a Faculty Startup fund of North West Agriculture and Forestry University and Shaanxi Postdoctoral Foundation (2023BSHYDZZ88) for the financial support. Authors would like to thank the instrument shared platform of college of food science & engineering of NWAFU for the assistance in the FT-IR analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.susmat.2025.e01448.
